original_abstract: This study identifies the core knowledge areas and professional competencies essential for executive leadership in higher education, as perceived by sitting university presidents. Through qualitative inquiry, thirteen presidents detailed the specific attributes they believe are foundational to an effective presidency. The findings indicate that essential knowledge domains include foundational administrative expertise, the acquisition of cultural intelligence, and complex cognitive processing. Furthermore, respondents identified personal attributes, strategic management, and sophisticated communication as the primary competency areas required for success. A significant outcome of the study reveals that university presidents view their roles as inherently dualistic, requiring them to function simultaneously as visionary leaders and high-level managers.
humanized_abstract: Using the perspective of sitting university presidents, this study identified core knowledge areas and competencies that executives in higher education must possess to lead their institutions successfully. Thirteen presidents described in qualitative terms the leadership competencies they believed are basic to the effective presidency. The findings of the study indicate that there are three core knowledge areas of foundational knowledge, cultural competence, and advanced-level cognitive skills. In addition, the presidents agreed that personal competencies, strategic planning, and effective communication are the major competencies that executives must have to be effective in the presidency. The study found that the presidency is a dichotomous office that calls upon executives to be at the same time charismatic leaders and chief administrative officers.

The study found that the presidency is a dichotomous office that calls upon executives to be at the same time charismatic leaders and chief administrative officers.
